Settings

No matching settings
Appearance
Off: only the Light/Dark switch above, changed by hand. System: follows your OS/browser's dark mode setting automatically. Schedule: switches at the hours you set below. In System or Schedule mode, clicking Light/Dark above still works — it holds as a temporary override until the schedule/system value naturally catches up and agrees with it again, then automatic switching resumes from there.
Dark starts at24-hour clock, 0–23
Light starts at24-hour clock, 0–23
100%
Background of the toolbar, sidebar, status bar, app bar, settings, help, and menus. Doesn't affect the editor/canvas writing surface.
Default
Bars
Status barShow the node count / depth / word count strip at the bottom. Also hideable via right-click on the status bar itself, with an Undo toast — this switch is the reliable way back if that toast is missed, since a hidden status bar has no space left to right-click on
Which individual items appear in the status bar strip, independent of the master Status bar switch above. The "⚠ Back up your data" reminder isn't included here — it only ever appears when your backup is genuinely overdue, so hiding it permanently would defeat the point; if it fires too often, raise the reminder interval in Settings → Data & backup instead.
Drag to reorder the right-hand cluster of the status bar (Saved status, backup indicators, theme, text size, author, and Quick Assist). Hidden items still keep their place in this list so the order is ready for when you turn them back on; the left-hand counters (node count, depth, word count) aren't reorderable — they stay in their fixed sequence.
Tab barShow the row of open-document tabs. Also hideable via right-click on empty tab bar space, with an Undo toast — this switch is the reliable way back if that toast is missed, for the same reason as Status bar above
App barShow the very top row (Documents/Templates, Settings, Help, Import/Export). Unlike Status bar and Tab bar above, this switch is not a reliable way back if you hide it and change your mind — Settings itself lives in this row, so once it's hidden you can't get back in here. Use Ctrl/Cmd+Shift+A, or right-click the editor canvas for "Show app bar," instead
Sidebar
New folders start collapsedApplies when you create a folder
Sidebar tipsBring back the dismissed right-click/drag hint
Filter boxTitle filter above the docs list (global search · Ctrl/Cmd+Space · covers docs, templates and node text)
Toolbar
Show toolbarQuick-access icons above the editor — also applies in zen mode; a floating button by the editor and Ctrl/Cmd+Shift+T mirror this switch
Group labelsCaption beneath each icon group
Word wrapWrap long lines in the editor. Turn off to scroll horizontally instead
ExpandedShow Extras as buttons instead of a menu
Customize toolbar buttonsAll buttons are shown
Hide any of the small buttons bottom-right of the editor (Zen, Pad, Toolbar, Preview) you don't use — the keyboard shortcut and the feature itself keep working either way, this only hides the button. For Pad, turning the whole feature off in Settings → Features hides their button too, regardless of this setting.
Pad
NotepadDocked panel for whole-document notes, separate from per-node notes. Toggle with the toolbar icon or Ctrl/Cmd+Shift+P
HeaderShow a title bar with its own close button above the toolbar
Match editor backgroundOn: Pad's writing area matches the outline's canvas. Off: it matches the sidebar/toolbar tone instead, to read as clearly separate from the document
Hide buttons you don't use in the Pad's toolbar
Note Panel
Backlinks@mention other nodes; existing [[mentions]] still render as links either way
Match editor backgroundOn: the note's writing area matches the outline's canvas. Off: it matches the sidebar/toolbar tone instead, to read as clearly separate from the document
Open notes in compact viewNotes open as a small popover with just the writing area and Rewrite — no backlinks, timestamps, or other toolbar buttons. Expand any individual note back to the full view with the compact button in its header; that choice doesn't persist past closing it
Hide buttons you don't use in the per-node note panel's toolbar
Code Block
Match editor backgroundOn: the code block's writing area matches the outline's canvas. Off: it matches the sidebar/toolbar tone instead, to read as clearly separate from the document
To-Dos
Dock in status barMove the To-Dos button down into the status bar instead of the app bar.
Overdue count badgeShow a small count badge on the To-Dos button itself when you have overdue tasks, so you can tell at a glance without opening the panel.
Row actions"On hover" keeps rows compact and reveals drag/priority/due/rewrite/delete only when you point at a row. "Always visible" keeps them in place permanently — less compact, but nothing moves or hides.
Auto-expire completed tasksAutomatically delete completed tasks after this long. "Never" keeps them until you delete them yourself.
Keep at mostOnce completed tasks exceed this count, the oldest completions are dropped first. "No limit" keeps everything until expiry or manual delete.
Right-click menu
Customize right-click menuAll actions are shown
Zen mode
Hide toolbar by defaultAutomatically hide the toolbar when entering zen mode. If you show it again manually while zen, it stays shown until you exit
Hide Pad by defaultAutomatically close the Pad panel when entering zen mode, and restore it on exit if it was open before
Hide File Explorer (sidebar)Automatically hide the sidebar when entering zen mode. The header's sidebar toggle button still works normally in zen mode and always overrides this — if you show it again manually while zen, it stays shown until you exit
Hide status barHide the bottom status bar entirely while in zen mode. Off by default — zen mode instead trims the status bar down to just Saved status and the auto-rewrite indicator (plus Quick Assist, if it's normally docked in the app bar — see "Hide app bar" below), regardless of your normal status bar item selection, and restores your normal selection on exit
Hide tab barHide the row of open document tabs while in zen mode. Off by default — tabs stay visible so you can switch between open documents without leaving zen mode
Hide app barAlso hide the very top row (Documents/Templates, Settings, Help, Import/Export) while in zen mode — just the tree (and toolbar, if it's on) remains. On by default. If Quick Assist is normally docked in the app bar ("Dock in app bar" in Settings → Quick Assist), zen mode relocates it into the status bar for as long as zen mode is on, instead of peeking the app bar back open every time you invoke it — your actual "Dock in app bar" preference is untouched and Quick Assist returns to the app bar when you exit zen mode
Use browser fullscreenAlso request the browser's native fullscreen (like pressing F11) when entering zen mode — hides the browser's own tabs and address bar too, not just Sakura's UI. On by default. Exiting fullscreen (Esc, F11, or the browser's own control) exits zen mode too, to keep the two in sync
Presets
Editor's choiceConfigures the toolbar, sidebar, Pad, hover toolbar, status bar, app bar, Quick Assist location, and editor backgrounds in one click for a leaner, writing-focused set: toolbar, sidebar, and Pad start hidden; hover toolbar is turned off; the status bar is trimmed to just Words, Depth, Saved status, and the auto-rewrite indicator (node count, max depth, node word count, the backup-connection chip, the theme chip, the text-size control, and the author field are hidden); the app bar stays visible with Quick Assist docked in it. Your accent color and node text color are left exactly as they are — this preset never touches either. Note, Code block, and Pad all switch off "Match editor background," so each reads as visually distinct chrome rather than blending into the outline canvas. The toolbar's own button set — Highlight, Text color, Collapse, Expand, Date/time, Decision log, Note, Code block, Tags, Rewrite, and Delete — is ready for whenever you bring the toolbar back; the right-click quick row gets Focus, Rewrite, Note, Code block, Date/time, Decision log, Tags, and Delete. Everything else is hidden — you can still adjust anything individually below afterward. Also runnable from Quick Assist as a Run action ("editor's choice" or "apply editor's choice") — it applies immediately, same as clicking Apply here, and its toast includes an Undo that restores every setting it touched.
Editing
Outline numberingNumber sections 1, 1.1, 1.1.1
Confirm before deleteAsk before removing nodes. Note: if the node is referenced by backlinks, a warning always shows regardless of this setting
SpellcheckUnderline misspelled words while typing
Date/time format
Default authorPre-fills the author on every new document, and any template's Date/Author line, so you don't have to set it each time. Existing documents are unaffected — change a single document's author from the status bar.
Node Quick Assist
Node Quick AssistWhile editing a node, Ctrl/Cmd+Space opens a small menu of fast node actions — insert em dash, date/time, note, code block, tags, decision log, rewrite — instead of the full Quick Assist. Off: Ctrl/Cmd+Space always opens the full Quick Assist, even mid-edit
Everything below configures what's in the mini menu once it's on. No typeahead — arrow keys + Enter, or click.
Icon-only rowShow the mini menu as a compact wrapping row of icons instead of a vertical list of icon+label rows. Hover an icon (or use arrow keys, same as before) to see its name. Same actions, same order — just a more compact layout.
Pick which fast actions appear in the mini menu while editing a node, and in what order — items always render in this fixed order regardless of which ones are on
Node hover toolbar
Hover ToolbarHover any node to reveal Menu and Zoom-in buttons next to it
Everything below configures what's in the Menu dropdown once it's on.
Pick which labeled actions appear in the dropdown when Menu is clicked. Zoom in on branch has its own dedicated button, so it isn't listed here. Defaults to the structural basics — add child, insert above/below.
Decision Log
Decision LogStructured decision entries — insert button, right-click action, and Excel export
Ask who decidedPrompt for a name each time you insert a decision log; timestamp is always added automatically
Color field labelsColor Context, Decision, Rationale, etc. in the editor so each field reads at a glance. Doesn't affect the Decision Log card in Preview, whose labels always stay neutral
Bold field labelsBold the field labels above, in addition to coloring them. Off keeps them the same weight as surrounding text — lighter, less shouty.
Pick which prompts get inserted under the header each time — turn off the ones you never fill in (Status defaults to "Proposed" and is the most commonly skipped). The header itself (timestamp + author) is always added
Focus
FocusZoom into a branch — toolbar button, right-click action, and Ctrl/Cmd+.
Preview
Always start fully expandedOff starts every Preview fully folded to the top level instead
Which filled-in fields appear in a decision log's card in Preview, each as its own labeled block — kept short so it stays readable, but a field with children (e.g. a numbered Rationale or bulleted Impact list) still shows that nested content in full. Status always shows as a badge in the card header instead. Doesn't affect what's actually in the document, or the Decision Log feature itself
Assign a heading level to a tree depth. In Preview, a node with no heading style of its own borrows the level assigned to its depth here. Depths left as "None" render as plain body text, exactly as they do today. Never overrides a heading you've actually applied in the editor.
Depth 0 (root)
Depth 1
Depth 2
Depth 3
Depth 4
Depth 5
Controls what shows up in the Contents panel and in the exported PDF's contents page. Doesn't affect the document itself.
Deepest level to includeHeadings below this level are left out of Contents, though they still render normally in the document. "All levels" includes everything.
Include section markersNodes written as [Section] act as top-level dividers in Preview. On by default; turn off to keep Contents to real headings only.
Visual options for Preview only — none of these touch the editor or your document.
Reading width
1080px
Sepia reading themeWarm, paper-like background for Preview, independent of light/dark mode elsewhere in the app.
Serif body textBody text renders in a serif typeface for reading; headings stay sans-serif for contrast.
PDF page marginsDefault margin size when exporting to PDF — the print dialog that opens still lets you override this per export.
Presenter slide breaks atWhich tree depth starts a new slide in Presenter mode. Depth 0 (default) means each top-level node is a slide; Depth 1 means each top-level node's direct children are, and so on.
Auto-enable laser pointerTurn the laser pointer on automatically when entering Presenter mode, and off when you exit.
Layout
Compact rowsTighter line spacing, independent of text size
Accent colour on branchesTint the tree branch connectors and lines with the active accent colour. Off by default — branches stay neutral.
Fade nested textDeeper nodes fade toward grey as you nest, as a visual hierarchy cue (matches WorkFlowy/Dynalist/Roam). Off: every node uses the same Text color regardless of depth — raises contrast on deeply-nested text at the cost of the at-a-glance depth cue
Hide tree linesClean indented view, no │├└─ connectors — like Dynalist/Workflowy. Toggle anytime with Ctrl/Cmd+Shift+L. Indentation and folding stay the same either way.
Timestamps on hoverHovering the tree connector or dot at the start of a node — not the text itself — shows when it was created and last modified. Turn off if the popup feels like clutter while you're just moving the cursor around.
Row selection styleHow selected rows are marked in the tree
Text size
100%
Horizontal indent width per depth level — higher values give more visual separation between parent and child nodes
Startup
Start each session blankNew document each time, instead of reopening the last one
Reopen tabs on launchRestore whichever documents were open as tabs last time, instead of just one
Quick Assist
Quick Assist search boxShow the search / Quick Assist box, currently docked in the status bar (Ctrl/Cmd+Space opens and focuses it regardless; Quick Assist can also hide it via a command like "hide search bar" — this switch is the reliable way to bring it back)
Dock in app barMove the Quick Assist box up into the app bar (left of Settings) instead of the status bar. Off keeps it in the status bar strip; on docks it beside Settings at the top instead — its position there isn't reorderable the way the status bar cluster is. Ctrl/Cmd+Space keeps working the same either way, and zen mode's "Hide app bar" / "Hide status bar" switches follow wherever it currently lives.
Icon onlyCollapse the Quick Assist box down to just its icon when idle, to save status bar space. Click the icon, or use Ctrl/Cmd+Space as usual, to expand it to the full input — it collapses back down the same way it already closes (Escape, clicking elsewhere, or running a command). Has no effect if the search box above is hidden entirely, and only applies while Quick Assist is docked in the status bar — it's a no-op while "Dock in app bar" above is on.
Show search resultsBelow command matches, also show matching documents, notes, tags, and other content. Turn off to keep Quick Assist to commands only.
Show example phrasesThe "Try things like…" tip shown at the bottom of Quick Assist when the box is empty. Turn off to hide it — Quick Assist itself keeps working the same either way.
Search results shownMaximum number of search hits, across all categories combined, shown below command matches
Pick which kinds of content and settings show up as "Go to" results in Quick Assist
Data
Backup all dataSaves every document, folder, and template to a single backup file
Restore from backupRestores from a backup file — replaces everything currently in the app
Auto-backup to fileWrites a live backup to a file on disk as you work. Chrome/Edge only — Safari and Firefox don't support this yet.
Local safety copyA second copy is kept automatically in a separate browser storage area, in case the main storage gets cleared. Not a substitute for Export.
Remind me to back upShows a status-bar warning if it's been longer than this since your last manual Export or auto-backup write.
Warn if this session isn't backed upStrict: the browser's own "leave site?" prompt on close attempt, then this dialog if you cancel it — a real last-chance gate, but you'll see the browser's generic popup. Passive: this dialog only, shown next time you switch away from or close the tab — no browser popup, but a direct close with no prior tab-switch gets no warning at all. Off: neither; the status-bar chip above is your only signal.
Why this mattersOpening a new version of this file starts with empty storage (browsers isolate it per file). Export before switching files, then Import right after to carry your data over. Auto-backup and the safety copy protect against accidental data loss, but only Export gives you a file outside the browser.
Reset to default settingsRestores appearance and behavior preferences (theme, toolbar, right-click menu, accent color, and the like) to their defaults. Doesn't touch your documents, folders, templates, or AI provider settings.
Restore built-in templatesRecreates any built-in template — or the Built-in folder — you deleted or renamed, using the original shipped content. Templates you duplicated or made yourself aren't touched.
Trash
Keep deleted documents & templates forDeleted documents and templates both go to the same Trash, not gone for good. After this long they're purged automatically — pick "Forever" to only ever remove them by emptying Trash yourself.
Include Trash in searchQuick Assist's search results (Ctrl/Cmd+Space) normally only look at active documents and templates. Turn this on to also surface matches sitting in Trash, clearly marked as such.
Empty TrashPermanently deletes every document and template currently in Trash. This cannot be undone.
Export & print
Skip folded sectionsOff: exports and printing always include everything. On: only what's currently expanded is included.
Cover page in PDFAdd a title page (document title, author, date) before the contents page when exporting to PDF.
Hide items you don't use from the header's Export ▾ menu
Provider
Provider
▾
Custom providers
All six built-in providers can’t be edited or removed. Every provider needs an API key — select one from the Provider dropdown above to see where to get it. GitHub Models requires a fine-grained personal access token with models:read scope. Add any additional OpenAI-compatible, Gemini-style, or Anthropic-style API with “+ Add provider” — user-added providers can be edited or deleted.
Model
▾
API Key
No key saved. No requests to this provider yet today.
Fallback orderWhen any error occurs on the active provider (quota, rate limit, or other failure), Sakura retries with the next checked provider in order. The active provider is always tried first. Providers without a saved key are skipped. A toast confirms which provider was used.
Reset AI settingsRestores provider, model, and rewrite prompt to their defaults, and clears any saved API key. Separate from the general settings reset — your documents and other app preferences aren't touched either way.
Rewrite
Rewrite max response lengthCaps output tokens for the Rewrite feature specifically (editor and Pad). Generate Outline (2048), Restructure Text (4096), and short tasks like Summarise/Tags already use their own larger or smaller caps tuned to what they do, and aren't affected by this. Raise this if rewriting a long selection gets cut off.
Rewrite prompt
Auto-rewrite on commit
Auto-rewrite on commitRuns Rewrite automatically on nodes as you finish editing them — no manual click. Queued and sent as a single batched request after a short pause or once enough nodes have queued up (both configurable below), not one request per node, to avoid burning through provider rate limits during continuous note-taking. Uses the same Rewrite prompt configured above — there's no separate prompt for auto-rewrite. Requires an API key configured above; turns itself off after repeated failures rather than retrying silently forever. Shows a live status in the bottom status bar whenever it's on.
Auto-rewrite exclusions — text matching any checked category is left alone, since "form a sentence" doesn't apply to it and rewriting it risks corrupting syntax Sakura parses specially. Unchecking a box lets auto-rewrite touch that category too.
Minimum word count
Nodes shorter than this are treated as labels, not sentences-in-progress, and skipped. Range 0–20; 0 disables the length check entirely.
Idle pause before sending
Seconds of no further edits before the queued nodes are sent as one batch. Lower sends sooner but in smaller batches; higher lets more nodes accumulate per request. Range 1–300 seconds.
Nodes queued before sending
If this many nodes queue up before the idle pause elapses, they're sent immediately rather than waiting — keeps a long capture session from growing the batch past what one request can handle well. Range 2–50.
Show queued count in chip
The status bar chip shows "N queued" while a batch is pending. Off: just shows that something's queued, without the number.
Show countdown in chip
The chip shows a live "sends in Xs" countdown to the next batch, resetting every time another node joins the queue. Off if you find the ticking distracting rather than useful.
Features
Master on/off switches for the bigger, optional capabilities. Turning one off hides every entry point for it — toolbar buttons, right-click menu items, and the matching Settings and Help sections.
Node content
Note PanelPer-node notes — toolbar button, right-click action, and the note panel itself
Code BlockPer-node code attachment with syntax highlighting — toolbar button, right-click action, and the code panel itself
Tagging#tags on nodes — insert button, right-click action, and the tag manager
Navigation & workspace
Pad PanelDocked whole-document notepad, separate from per-node notes
To-DosApp-level task list, separate from documents — app bar/status bar button and the To-Dos panel itself
Quick AssistCtrl/Cmd+Space command box — type plain-English instructions like "hide sidebar" and they run immediately, run one-off actions like "new document" or "duplicate node," run AI actions like "rewrite document" or "expand node" (each reuses the same AI feature as its toolbar button, so it needs an API key configured under AI Rewrite and respects AI Capabilities being on), or type anything else to search documents, notes, tags, settings, and help. Toggles, one-off actions, and settings changes are instant and reversible with Undo; AI actions are the exception — they call the configured AI provider and can take a few seconds or fail like any other AI call, but their results are still added to Undo once they land
Automation
AI CapabilitiesRewrite, expand, summarise, generate outline, suggest tags/icon, and Restructure text (Import menu) — the AI toolbar group and matching right-click/import items. Word import's automatic fallback for documents with no heading structure also needs this on
Full
Keyboard Shortcuts
Customize shortcutsAll shortcuts are at their defaults
Everything here is stored locally in your browser — nothing leaves this tab.

Help

No matching help topics
New here? Take the guided tour.
Quick reference for everything below.
Curious why an outliner helps?
See it all come together, end to end.
Pick a category on the left for reference on any part of Sakura — shortcuts, panels, AI, and everything else.

Core editing

  • Enter / F2 → edit
  • Enter → new sibling below (while editing)
  • Shift + Enter → split at the cursor — text after the cursor moves into a new sibling below, text before it stays put
  • Ctrl/Cmd + Enter → new child — works whether you're editing a node or just have one selected
  • Type → start editing

Navigation

  • Click → select
  • Shift + Click → range
  • Ctrl/Cmd + Click → multi-select
  • Ctrl/Cmd + A → select all

Quick Assist

  • Lives in the header, where a plain search box used to sit. Ctrl/Cmd + Space focuses it and opens the results dropdown from anywhere in the app; clicking directly into the box does the same. Type a plain-English instruction and it's carried out immediately: "get me toolbar," "I don't want sidebar," "get rid of pad," "toggle dark mode" — or just type anything else to search, the same as the old separate Global Search box (formerly Ctrl/Cmd+K, now folded into this one shortcut and box) did
  • To search just one category instead of all eleven at once, type a category prefix before your query — "notes: budget" only searches Notes, "settings: dark" only searches Settings. Short aliases work too (doc/docs, note/notes, tag/tags, feature/features, and so on). Don't want to remember the exact word? Click the icon at the left of the box, or press Space while it's completely empty — either shows a picker inline in the dropdown, navigable the same way search results are (↓ / ↑ to move, Enter to pick). Above the eleven categories, the same picker also lists Show, Hide, Toggle, and Run — picking one inserts that bare word instead of a category prefix, which does something different: rather than scoping a search, it browses everything of that kind (see the bare-verb bullet below). Either way, choosing an entry leaves the dropdown open, cursor ready for you to keep typing and narrow further. A category prefix with nothing typed after it (just "notes: " on its own) shows no results rather than browsing the whole category — the box doesn't have a "show me everything in here" mode for categories, only "search within here," so you still need something to search for after a category prefix specifically; the four verb words above are the exception; each of those does browse everything on its own with nothing else typed
  • Typing just a bare word — "show," "hide," "toggle," or "run," nothing else — lists everything of that kind instead of requiring you to already know a specific setting's name: "show" lists everything currently hidden that could be shown, "hide" lists everything currently shown that could be hidden, "toggle" lists everything either way regardless of its current state, and "run" lists every available Run action ("new document," "duplicate node," "insert decision log," "editor's choice preset," and the AI actions covered below). Keep typing after the bare word to narrow the list further by name — "show tool" narrows to just toolbar-related results, the same as typing "show toolbar" would, just built up in two steps instead of one
  • On a Mac, if the system's own Spotlight is bound to Cmd+Space (its default), it takes priority over the browser and this shortcut won't fire — either change Spotlight's shortcut in macOS System Settings → Keyboard, or remap this one from Settings → Keyboard Shortcuts
  • Toggles and search are rule-based, not AI — a fixed set of recognized phrases (show/get/enable/turn on… vs. hide/remove/get rid of/turn off…) matched against a fixed list of settings. Nothing is sent anywhere for these, no API key needed, and it never improvises — if it doesn't recognize a phrase, it says so rather than guessing. The AI Run actions below are the one exception: those do call your configured AI provider, the same as clicking their toolbar/right-click equivalent would
  • Covers the everyday visibility toggles: toolbar (and its group labels), sidebar (and its filter box), Pad, zen mode, dark/light mode, auto-rewrite on commit, tree lines, timestamps on hover, spellcheck, backlinks, compact rows, word wrap, outline numbering, the Quick Assist search box itself and its icon-only mode, the hover toolbar, delete confirmation, decision log colors, accent-colored branches, expanded toolbar, and the startup tab/blank-session preferences — plus every on/off switch for a whole capability, whether it lives in Settings → Features (Note Panel, Code Block, Tagging, AI Capabilities, Pad Panel, To-Dos, Quick Assist) or inside that panel's own section (Decision Log, Focus, Backlinks, Hover Toolbar, Node Quick Assist). It does not cover destructive actions (reset, delete, empty trash) or multi-value settings (themes, sliders) — those still live in Settings, on purpose
  • Note Panel, Code Block, Tagging, and Decision Log are reachable with slightly longer phrases than you might expect — "note panel feature," "code block feature," "tags feature," "decision log feature" — rather than their bare names. That's deliberate: those bare words are also what Quick Assist searches for as content (or, for Decision Log, what every inserted entry starts with), so a short keyword there would rank a toggle above the search hit you were actually looking for. Pad Panel is qualified too ("pad feature") for a different reason — bare "pad" is already claimed by the command that opens or closes the Pad panel for this session, which is a separate thing from turning the whole feature on or off
  • Below command matches, a separate row type labeled Run covers one-off actions instead of toggles: "new document," "duplicate node," "duplicate," "insert decision log" (or "add decision log"), and "editor's choice preset" (or "apply editor's choice") — or type the bare word "run" with nothing else to list all of them at once, the same way a bare "show" or "hide" lists every matching toggle. These don't have an on/off state to show or an existing value to report — they just do the thing once, the same as clicking the equivalent toolbar or Settings button would. Only safe, additive, undoable operations are ever reachable this way; nothing destructive is offered as a Run action, same rule as the toggles above. "Duplicate node" and "Insert decision log" need a node selected first — if nothing's selected, they still show up (so you know they exist) but greyed out with "select a node first" instead of a working button, rather than either hiding them or letting a click do nothing unexplained. Undo means different things per action: for "new document," "duplicate node," and "insert decision log" it's the regular document Undo; "editor's choice preset" instead restores every setting it touched (toolbar, sidebar, Pad, hover toolbar, accent, text color, right-click menu) back to what they were immediately before, since none of those live on the document undo stack
  • AI Run actions — also listed under "run," these are the exception to "rule-based, not AI" above: "rewrite document" (the whole open document, same as right-click → Rewrite document), "rewrite this node" / "rewrite selected" (current selection, single or multiple nodes), "expand node" / "expand into subtree" (needs exactly one node selected), "summarise selection" (needs 2 or more nodes selected), "suggest tags" (needs exactly one node selected), "generate outline" (opens the same topic-prompt dialog as its toolbar button), and "restructure text" (opens the same paste-text dialog). Each needs an API key configured in Settings → AI → Provider and disappears entirely if AI Capabilities (Settings → Features) is off — same requirement as the toolbar buttons they reuse. Unlike the rule-based actions above, these take a moment to run and can fail (expired key, provider rate limit) exactly like clicking their toolbar equivalent would — a missing key or a failed call surfaces the same error dialog either way, not a silent no-op. Undo works the regular way once a result lands, since each reuses the exact same function — and therefore the exact same pushUndo() call — as its toolbar/right-click counterpart
  • Below any command and action matches, the same box also surfaces matching documents, node text, notes, tags, the pad, and settings/help topics — covering settings, help, document and template names, node text across every document (open or not), per-node Notes, per-node Code blocks, tags, and each document's Pad. Decision Log entries are just regular node text under the hood, so they're already covered under "In documents" — no separate category needed for them. These rows are labeled Go to instead of Show/Hide, since picking one navigates you there rather than changing anything, and each shows a short snippet of where the match sits so you're not guessing what you're about to open
  • Clicking a "Go to" result does the right thing for its type: a node-text match jumps to that node and highlights the match; a Notes match opens that node's Note panel; a Tags match jumps to the tagged node; a Pad match opens that document's Pad
  • Skips Trash by default. Settings → Trash → "Include Trash in search" surfaces matches there too, across every content type above, clearly marked "In Trash"; clicking one reveals it in the Trash section rather than opening it directly, since restoring is a deliberate choice
  • Rich-text fields (Notes, Pad) are stripped to plain text before matching, so a search never hits raw HTML markup and snippets always show readable text
  • The sidebar's own filter box only matches document/template names, not their contents — for anything inside a document, this box is the one that actually looks
  • Settings → Workspace → Quick Assist controls the search side of this specifically: a switch to turn search results off entirely (command-only), how many results show at once, and which categories (Documents, Notes, Tags, Settings, Help, and so on) are eligible to appear at all
  • As you type, the list runs in a fixed order top to bottom — matching commands first, then Run actions, then search results — each labeled with what it'll do ("Hide Sidebar," "Show Toolbar," "Run: New document," "Go to: …") and, for toggles, the setting's current state; ↓ / ↑ moves through the combined list (disabled Run rows are skipped, since there's nothing to activate), Enter runs or opens whichever is highlighted, Esc closes and clears the box, or click any of them directly
  • Every command and Run action shows a toast with an Undo button — nothing is ever silently applied. Just typing a bare toggle name with no verb ("toolbar," "sidebar") flips its current state rather than doing nothing. If a Run action has its own confirmation step (Insert decision log asks who's deciding first) and you cancel that step, nothing happens and the toast says so instead of falsely claiming success. "Go to" rows are navigation, not a change to anything, so there's nothing to undo for those
  • If a phrase matches more than one setting, all of them show up as separate ranked options instead of Quick Assist silently picking one for you

Node Quick Assist

  • Ctrl/Cmd + Space while actively editing a node opens a small menu of node-specific actions instead of the full Quick Assist — same shortcut, different box, depending on whether you're mid-edit. Everywhere else, it opens the full Quick Assist as usual
  • Default actions, in order: Insert em dash, Insert en dash, Insert arrow, Insert check mark, Insert cross mark, Insert middle dot, Insert date/time, Note…, Code block…, Tags…, Insert decision log, Rewrite node. Which of these appear (and their fixed order) is configured in Settings → Editing → Node Quick Assist → Menu items
  • No typeahead — unlike @mentions or the full Quick Assist, this menu doesn't filter as you type. ↓ / ↑ moves through it and wraps at either end, Enter runs the highlighted action, Esc or resuming normal typing dismisses it. There's deliberately nowhere for a typed query to live except the node's own text, which this avoids touching
  • Settings → Editing → Node Quick Assist → Icon-only row switches the menu from a vertical icon+label list to a compact wrapping row of icons — same actions, same order, same keyboard navigation, just a smaller footprint. Hover an icon for its name
  • Insert em dash, Insert en dash, Insert arrow, Insert check mark, Insert cross mark, and Insert date/time all splice directly at the cursor and keep you in edit mode, so you don't lose your place — the fastest actions in the menu, and the reason it exists. Note, Code block, Tags, and Insert decision log commit the edit first and open their normal panel, exactly as right-click → the same item would. Rewrite node runs the same AI rewrite as the toolbar/right-click version, including its own "Rewritten ✦" confirmation toast
  • The popup and any panel it opens (Tags, Note, Code block) position themselves off the actual cursor location, not just the node row — accurate even on a long, wrapped node where the cursor isn't near the top of the row
  • Note, Code block, Tags, Decision log, and Rewrite node each drop out of the menu automatically if their own feature is turned off (Settings → Features), same as everywhere else those actions appear
  • Settings → Editing → Node Quick Assist turns the whole thing off — Ctrl/Cmd+Space then always opens the full Quick Assist, even mid-edit, same as before this feature existed

Appearance & Display

  • Theme & accent — Settings → Appearance: Light/Dark theme, seven accent colours, and an intensity slider. Auto theme sits below it — Off (the Light/Dark switch only, changed by hand), System (follows your OS/browser's dark mode setting live, no polling needed), or Schedule (switches at hours you set, checked once a minute plus whenever the tab regains focus, to catch changes that happened while it was backgrounded or the machine was asleep). Manually clicking Light/Dark in System or Schedule mode holds as a temporary override until the schedule/system value naturally catches up and agrees with it again — e.g. forcing dark during a scheduled light window holds through the next scheduled dark transition and then continues following the schedule normally from there, rather than reverting within the next check. "Accent colour on branches" (Settings → Layout) tints the tree connector lines with the accent too; off by default so branches stay neutral
  • Chrome background (Settings → Appearance, below Accent) — five presets: Default (follows the Light/Dark theme), Slate, Sand, Ink, and Rose. Recolors the toolbar, sidebar, status bar, app bar, settings, help, and menus; the editor/canvas writing surface is untouched, so your outline keeps reading against its own background regardless of which preset is picked. Independent of both the accent color and the Light/Dark theme — any combination of the three is valid
  • Text color (Settings → Appearance, below Chrome background) — four presets for node text specifically: Default, Black, Charcoal, and Slate. Separate from the accent color (which colors buttons, borders, and highlights, not node text) and from Chrome background (which colors app chrome, not the outline itself)
  • Status bar items (Settings → Bars & Menus, under the Status bar switch) — which individual pieces show in the bottom strip: node count, max depth, word count, current depth, current node's word count, saved status, backup connection status, the auto-rewrite indicator, theme chip, text size control, and author field, each independently hideable. The master Status bar switch above still controls the whole strip; this is finer-grained, for keeping only what you actually use. The "⚠ Back up your data" reminder chip alone isn't in this list on purpose — it's an actionable alert that only appears when your backup is genuinely overdue, and permanently hiding an alert defeats it; if it fires too often, raise the reminder interval in Settings → Data & backup instead
  • Status bar item order (Settings → Bars & Menus, right below Status bar items) — drag to reorder the right-hand cluster: saved status, backup connection status, the backup-overdue reminder, the auto-rewrite indicator, theme chip, text size control, author field, and Quick Assist. Hidden items keep their place in this list, so turning one back on doesn't reset where it sits. The left-hand counters (node count, depth, word count) are a separate, fixed-order cluster and aren't part of this — only the right side is reorderable
  • Toolbar — Settings → Bars & Menus → Toolbar: "Show toolbar" hides/shows the icon bar (also toggled by the floating button next to the editor or Ctrl/Cmd + Shift + T); "Group labels" adds a caption under each icon group; "Word wrap" controls whether long lines wrap in the editor or scroll horizontally instead; "Expanded" shows the Extras group as buttons instead of a menu. "Sections" lets you hide whole icon groups (History, Structure, Move, Format, Fold, Insert, AI, Extras, Delete) for a leaner bar, and "Insert buttons" trims individual buttons within Insert without hiding the whole group
  • Editor's choice preset — Settings → Bars & Menus → Presets → Apply, or Quick Assist as a Run action ("editor's choice" — see below): one click reconfigures the toolbar, sidebar, Pad, hover toolbar, status bar, app bar, Quick Assist location, and editor backgrounds to a curated, writing-focused set. Toolbar, sidebar, and Pad start hidden; hover toolbar is turned off; the status bar keeps only Words, Depth, Saved status, and the auto-rewrite indicator, hiding node count, max depth, node word count, the backup-connection chip, the theme chip, the text-size control, and the author field; the app bar stays visible with Quick Assist docked in it. Your accent color and node text color are left untouched — this preset is about density and visibility, not color. Note, Code block, and Pad all switch "Match editor background" off, so each reads as visually distinct chrome rather than blending into the outline canvas. The toolbar's own button set is curated for whenever you bring it back (Highlight, Text color, Collapse, Expand, Date/time, Decision log, Note, Code block, Tags, Rewrite, and Delete); the right-click quick row gets Focus, Rewrite, Note, Code block, Date/time, Decision log, Tags, and Delete — Focus lands there specifically because this preset turns off the hover toolbar, its usual home. Everything else is hidden; you can still adjust any of this individually afterward in Settings → Bars & Menus (Toolbar / Right-click menu), or elsewhere in Settings. Clicking Apply here again just re-runs the same curated set and does not undo manual tweaks; running it from Quick Assist instead gives you an Undo button on the toast that restores exactly what was in place immediately before
  • Hide tree lines — Settings → Layout → "Hide tree lines" (or Ctrl/Cmd + Shift + L anytime): drops the │├└─ connector characters for a clean indented view, closer to Dynalist or Workflowy. Nodes with children keep their ▾/▸ fold arrow; childless nodes get a small dot in its place, so every row still has a marker. Indentation and folding behave identically either way — this only changes how the tree is drawn, not the structure itself
  • Row selection style — Settings → Layout → "Row selection style" controls how selected rows are marked: Fill (default) shades the row background, with the primary/anchor row a shade darker than the rest of a multi-select; Outline traces a thin border, with the anchor row's border and a light fill slightly stronger; Left bar shows an accent-coloured bar on the left edge, thicker on the anchor row; Dot marks each selected row with a small dot, larger and solid on the anchor row. Fill and Outline are neutral/near-neutral; Left bar and Dot use the active accent colour
  • Layout — Settings → Layout: "Compact rows" tightens line spacing independent of text size; "Text size" is a slider from 85% to 140%; "Branch spacing" sets the horizontal indent per depth level (2-6); "Collapse depth" sets how many levels stay expanded when you collapse all (1-6)
  • Sidebar — Settings → Bars & Menus → Sidebar: control whether new folders start collapsed, and whether a title-filter box appears above the documents list
  • All of the above are saved automatically and restored next time you open the app. "Reset to default settings" (Settings → Data) reverts appearance and behaviour preferences to their defaults without touching your documents

Structure

  • Right-click a node → a row of icon buttons for the most common actions; defaults are Zoom in, Note, Tags, Rewrite, Decision Log, Delete. Click More for move, sort, rewrite document, and everything else. Customise which buttons appear via Settings → Right-click menu — you can also reset to defaults there. Zoom in is also in the toolbar Fold group and via Ctrl/Cmd + .; zoom out via Ctrl/Cmd + , or clicking the same toolbar button again
  • Right-clicking empty space — editor canvas, sidebar, status bar, tab bar, or app bar — opens a separate, smaller menu of chrome-level actions, not the node menu above. Navigate it with ↓ / ↑ and Enter, same as the category picker above, or hover with a mouse — both move the same highlight. Editor canvas: switch theme, hide/show toolbar, sidebar, Pad, status bar, or tab bar, hide/show tree lines, "Show app bar" (only appears while zen mode has it hidden), Collapse to depth, Expand all, and Settings. Sidebar: switch theme, hide sidebar, toggle compact rows, Settings. Status bar: switch theme, hide status bar, Settings. Tab bar: switch theme, new document, close current tab, hide tab bar, Settings. App bar: switch theme, hide/show sidebar or toolbar, and "Declutter app bar" — which hides the title field, sidebar toggle, and the Documents/Templates buttons, but deliberately never hides Settings — hiding it would remove your only way back in. (Quick Assist used to share this exemption when it lived in the app bar too; it's since moved to the status bar, so it isn't part of this menu's options at all anymore.) Every hide action here shows an Undo toast, same pattern as Quick Assist's own toggles. If you hide Status bar or Tab bar from their own right-click menu, that specific spot disappears with it — but the editor canvas's own right-click menu keeps the same Status bar/Tab bar toggles, so it works as a fallback, in addition to the plain switches under Settings → Bars & Menus. Declutter app bar has no separate switch by design, since Settings (reachable via the button it deliberately never hides) already reverses it in one click
  • Backlinks — while editing a node, type @ to mention another node by name; a dropdown filters as you type, pick with mouse or arrow keys + Enter. The reference renders as a clickable link that jumps to the target. Open the Note panel on any node to see which nodes link to it. Deleting a referenced node removes its links from all other nodes automatically
  • Tags — select a node and click the tag icon (toolbar Insert group) or right-click → Tags to open the tag popover. Existing document tags appear as chips to toggle; type a new name and press Enter to add. Tags render as #chips on the node. Click a chip in the tree to filter to that tag; click again or × to clear. Multi-select: toolbar button applies to all selected nodes
  • Checkboxes — click the ☑ toolbar button (Insert group) to toggle a node as a checkbox. Or type [ ] / [x] at the start of a node while editing — it auto-converts on commit. Click the box to check/uncheck. Parent checkbox nodes with checkbox children show a 2/5 progress badge; checking all children auto-checks the parent. Checked nodes fade. Multi-select supported
  • ⤴ → insert above
  • ＋ → add child
  • Tab / Shift + Tab → indent / outdent
  • ⧉ → duplicate node / subtree
  • Alt + ↑ / ↓ → move
  • Drag a row → drop above/below to reorder, or onto the middle of another row to nest as its child
  • Right-click a node with children → More → Sort children (A → Z, Z → A, or by depth — shallowest subtree first)
  • Extras in the toolbar → sort all top-level nodes the same three ways

Tagging

  • Select a node and click the tag icon (toolbar Insert group) or right-click → Tags to open the tag popover. Existing document tags appear as chips to toggle; type a new name and press Enter to add. Remove a tag from a node by toggling its chip off in the popover
  • Tags render as #chips directly on the node row. Click a chip in the tree to filter the entire tree to that tag; click the chip again or the × to clear the filter
  • Multi-select: select several nodes, then click the tag icon — the popover applies to all selected nodes at once. Tags already on all selected nodes appear toggled on; tags on only some appear in an indeterminate state
  • AI Suggest tags (AI toolbar group) reads the node text and the document's existing tags, then applies relevant ones directly — prefers reusing tags already in the document before inventing new ones

@ Mentions & Backlinks

  • While editing a node, type @ to mention another node by name; a dropdown filters as you type — pick with mouse or arrow keys + Enter. The reference is saved as [[Node name]] and renders as a clickable link that jumps to the target node
  • Open the Note panel on any node to see its Backlinks section — every node that @mentions it appears there as a clickable entry. If no backlinks exist the section is hidden
  • Deleting a referenced node automatically removes its [[mention]] links from all other nodes
  • Turning the feature off (Settings → Panels → Note Panel → Backlinks) suppresses the @ dropdown and hides the Backlinks section in the note panel. Existing [[mentions]] in node text still render as clickable links regardless — they navigate on click even when the feature is off

Focus (zoom into a branch)

  • Right-click a node → Zoom in (or Ctrl/Cmd + ., or the zoom icon in the toolbar Fold group) → only that node and its descendants are shown. A breadcrumb bar appears above the editor showing the path back to the root; click any crumb to zoom out to that level
  • Exit focus with Ctrl/Cmd + ,, by clicking the same toolbar button again, or by clicking the root crumb in the breadcrumb bar
  • Focus state is per-tab and is not saved — closing and reopening a document always starts at the full tree
  • Turning the feature off (Settings → Editing → Focus) hides the zoom-in toolbar button and right-click action, and exits any active focus session immediately. Ctrl/Cmd + . also stops working while the feature is off

Node hover toolbar

  • Hover any node to reveal two small buttons — Menu and Zoom in — right next to that row's own bullet, at any depth, including top-level nodes. They fade in only while you're hovering, and don't require selecting the node first
  • Zoom in acts immediately, same as right-click → Zoom in on branch (or Ctrl/Cmd + .) — see the Focus section above
  • Menu opens a vertical list of actions with icons and labels — Add child, Insert above, Insert below by default. Pick one to act on the node you were hovering; the list closes automatically afterward. Close it without choosing anything via Esc, clicking elsewhere, or moving to a different node
  • Settings → Editing → "Node hover toolbar" turns the whole thing on or off, right at the top of that section — "Menu items" below it is a checklist of everything the Menu dropdown can show — structural actions, AI actions, Note/Code/Tags/Decision log, and Delete. Zoom in isn't in that list since it already has its own dedicated button
  • The fold/unfold arrow (▾/▸) on any node with children turns into −/+ while you hover it, and clicking it works exactly the same either way
  • Hovering the tree connector or the leaf dot at the start of a node — not the label text — shows a themed tooltip with when it was created and last modified. It's a separate control from the hover toolbar above; turn it off independently via Settings → Layout → "Timestamps on hover"

Formatting

  • Ctrl/Cmd + B → bold
  • Ctrl/Cmd + I → italic
  • Ctrl/Cmd + U → underline
  • Ctrl/Cmd + Shift + S → strike
  • Ctrl/Cmd + Shift + H → highlight (caret next to it picks a color)
  • Ctrl/Cmd + Shift + F → text color (caret next to it picks a color)
  • Ctrl/Cmd + Alt + 1–6 → apply Heading 1–6 to the selected node(s), independent of tree depth — pick from the dropdown next to Text color, or Ctrl/Cmd + Alt + 0 / re-clicking the same level to return to body text. Combines with bold/italic/underline/strike on top if you want further emphasis

Semantics

  • [Text] → Section — blue label
  • (Text) → note — muted italic
  • !Text → alert — red warning
  • `Text` → code — inline code
  • >Text → quoted or attributed content — starts the node with >; hides the > in view mode. Use for direct quotes, attributed statements, or cited requirements
  • @Node name → Node name — backlink to another node; type @ while editing to pick from a list. Open the Note panel on the target node to see all backlinks pointing to it
  • [ ] task or [x] task → auto-converts to a checkbox node on commit
  • Tag icon in toolbar or right-click → Tags → tag popover (toggle existing tags or add new ones)

AI Rewrite

  • ✦ button in the toolbar → rewrites the selected node; with multiple nodes selected, rewrites all of them in one batch; with text highlighted inside an editing node, rewrites just that selection
  • Right-click a node → Rewrite node is in the quick icon row (Rewrite selection, if multiple are selected); click More → Rewrite document to rewrite every node in the tree
  • button next to Rewrite, or Ctrl/Cmd + Shift + O → Generate outline (AI): describe a topic and it inserts an AI-generated nested outline into the current document — into a blank doc, or after the current selection; also available as "Generate with AI" on the blank-document screen. Uses the same provider/model/key as Rewrite (Settings → AI → Provider), but its own fixed instructions, not the customizable rewrite prompt
  • Restructure text (AI) — Import ▾ → "Restructure text", or Ctrl/Cmd + Shift + R: paste messy or unstructured text (notes, an email, a transcript) and it organizes it into a tree, cleaning up wording as needed (fixing broken line wraps, tightening long sentences) so it reads well as outline nodes — it will not invent facts that are not in your text, but it is not a verbatim copy either. If the pasted text already has clear structure (bullets, consistent indentation, or literal tree-connector characters), it is parsed directly with no AI call at all. Unlike Generate outline, this always opens as a new document rather than inserting into the one you have open, so an import can never accidentally merge into unrelated work — your original pasted text is kept in that new document's Pad so you can check it against the result
  • Word import — Import ▾ → "Word (.docx)": converts the file's own heading and list structure directly into nodes — no AI involved when the document actually uses heading styles, since that structure is already explicit in the file. If a document has no heading styles at all (just a wall of plain paragraphs), its text is automatically handed to the same AI restructuring used above instead of being imported as one long flat list. Tables import as a node per row with each column as a child node; images are dropped but left as a "[image]" placeholder so nothing vanishes silently. Always opens as a new document, with the extracted text kept in its Pad. Legacy .doc files (pre-2007 binary format) are not supported — only modern .docx
  • button next to Outline → Suggest icon: picks a fitting emoji for the selected node's label and adds it as a prefix (e.g. "🛒 Webshop"). With multiple nodes selected, applies one straight away to each, picked from a keyword list first and an exact match against icons already used elsewhere in your documents/templates, falling to AI only when neither matches. For a single node, if more than one good option turns up, a small popover appears above it to pick from; right-click → More → Suggest icons for all nodes runs it over the whole tree (asks to confirm first). Since the icon is just leading text, removing or changing it is normal editing — double-click the node and edit it like any other text
  • Batches larger than 30 nodes are sent in sequential chunks automatically, with a status toast showing progress (e.g. "Rewriting nodes 1–30…")
  • Expand node (AI) — select a single node and click the expand button in the AI toolbar group to break it into child nodes. Useful for dense labels like "Distribution model (OPML, localStorage, multi-tab sync)" → a proper subtree. AI generates 3–8 children; result is undoable
  • Suggest tags (AI) — select a node and click the tag-suggest button; AI reads the node text and the document's existing tags, then applies relevant ones directly. Prefers reusing tags already in the document before inventing new ones
  • Summarise selection into parent (AI) — select 2 or more nodes (Shift-click or Ctrl/Cmd-click), then click the summarise button; AI writes a concise parent label and inserts it above the selection, with the selected nodes indented underneath. The bottom-up outlining pattern — build the detail first, name the group after
  • Settings → AI → Provider → choose a provider from the dropdown, paste the API key for it, then head to the Rewrite section to optionally customise the rewrite prompt. All AI features — Rewrite, Expand, Tag suggestion, Summarise, Generate outline, Suggest icon — share the same active provider and key. "Reset to default" next to the prompt box restores the original grammar-and-spelling-only wording; "Rephrase" swaps in a different canned prompt that's allowed to restructure sentences and vary wording, not just correct errors — both replace the box's contents and save immediately, the same way, so review before relying on either. There's only one active prompt: switching to Rephrase also changes what Auto-rewrite on commit does, if it's on, since auto-rewrite always uses whichever prompt is currently active. "Reset AI settings" at the bottom restores provider, model, and prompt together and clears every saved key
  • Auto-rewrite on commit (Settings → AI → Auto-rewrite on commit, off by default) — runs Rewrite automatically on a node as soon as you finish editing it, no manual click needed. Only text typed by hand counts: if any part of an edit session came from a paste or drop (checked via the browser's own paste/drop signal on that edit), that node is skipped for auto-rewrite on that commit — paste it, then use the manual Rewrite button if you want AI on it. Committing a node with no actual text change (e.g. clicking in and back out without editing) is also skipped — it doesn't re-queue nodes that haven't changed. It doesn't send one request per node: committed nodes are queued and sent as a single batched request once either an idle pause passes or enough nodes have queued up, whichever comes first — both the pause length (1-300s, default 4s) and the queue size (2-50, default 12) are configurable in Settings, not fixed — built this way specifically to avoid exhausting provider rate limits during continuous note-taking. A small ✦ dot appears next to any node currently queued (or mid-batch, waiting on the response) — click that dot to pull just that node back out of the queue before it's sent — separate from the status bar chip, which shows the overall off / idle / queued-count-with-a-live-countdown / rewriting state and doubles as the control itself: click it to turn auto-rewrite on or off directly, right-click it for its settings (exclusions, minimum word count, idle pause, queue size). The countdown ticks down to the batch's send time and resets to the full idle-pause value every time another node joins the queue — a fresh edit means a fresh wait, not a shorter one. Both the queued count and the countdown are independently hideable from the chip (Settings → AI → Auto-rewrite on commit, below the queue-size setting) if either feels like more detail than you want at a glance — the chip still distinguishes "something's queued" from idle even with both turned off, just without the numbers; the tooltip on hover always shows the full detail regardless of what the chip itself displays. Exclusions (checkboxes, decision-log field lines, headings, [[backlinks]]/`code` syntax) and the minimum word count are each independently configurable checkboxes in Settings, not fixed. Needs an API key configured above to do anything; if it fails three times in a row (e.g. an expired key), it turns itself off and tells you why, rather than silently retrying forever. Switching documents discards anything still queued rather than rewriting into the wrong document. Nodes created by Restructure text, Generate outline, paste-a-tree, and Word/OPML import are never queued for auto-rewrite regardless of this setting — only nodes you type into interactively go through commitEdit at all
  • Built-in providers — six providers ship with Sakura: Gemini (free), Groq (free, fast), Claude API, OpenRouter (free models), Cerebras (free tier), and GitHub Models (free, needs your GitHub account). All need an API key — selecting a provider shows a direct link to where you get one. GitHub Models requires a GitHub personal access token with models:read scope; model IDs in the dropdown already include the required vendor prefix (e.g. openai/gpt-4o). Built-ins can’t be edited or deleted
  • Provider fallback — Settings → AI → Provider → Fallback order: enable the toggle to let Sakura automatically retry with the next checked provider when the active one fails — quota, rate limit, wrong model ID, or any other error. The active provider is always tried first; the list sets the retry order. Providers without a saved key are skipped automatically. A toast names both the failed provider and the one that succeeded (e.g. “Quota hit on Groq — rewritten with Gemini ✦” or “Error on Groq — rewritten with Gemini ✦”). The only error that does not trigger fallback is a bad API key (401) — that surfaces directly so you know to update it. Default order on a fresh install: Groq → Gemini → Cerebras → OpenRouter, with the first three enabled
  • Usage today — below the API key field, Sakura counts requests made to the currently selected provider today (and shows the count next to each provider in the Fallback order list once any requests are made). This is a local, best-effort counter — it resets on your computer's local calendar day, which can drift slightly from a provider's own reset clock (e.g. Gemini resets at midnight Pacific), and it isn't fetched from the provider itself. Treat it as a rough gauge of how much you've used a provider today, not an authoritative quota reading — check the provider's own dashboard (e.g. Google AI Studio) for the real number if it matters
  • Test key — the Test button next to Save sends a minimal request (asks the model to reply "OK") to confirm the currently typed or saved key actually works, without waiting for your next real Rewrite to find out. A toast reports success or the specific error. This check does not count toward the Usage today total above, since it's a connectivity check rather than real feature use
  • Custom providers — "+ Add provider" connects any OpenAI-compatible, Gemini-style, or Anthropic-style API by name and base URL — no code changes. User-added providers can be edited or deleted; built-ins cannot. Each provider remembers its own last-used model independently

Utilities

  • Ctrl/Cmd + S → save now (kept in-app — doesn't trigger the browser's "Save Page As")
  • Print button in the header (next to Import ▾ / Export ▾) → prints the document with all UI chrome hidden; choose "Save as PDF" in the browser's print dialog to get a clean PDF. Checked nodes and tag chips render correctly in print; the sidebar, toolbar, and status bar are all hidden automatically
  • Export ▾ is grouped into Copy / Document / Data / AI sections. Everything in it respects Settings → Export & print → "Skip folded sections" the same way — folded branches are included or skipped consistently across Markdown, Word, OPML, and the items below, not just whichever one you happen to use
  • Import ▾ (next to Export ▾) is grouped into "From a file" (OPML, Word) and "From pasted text" (Restructure text, AI) — see the AI section above for how Restructure text and Word import handle structure
  • Diagram ✦ (Export ▾ → AI) — copies a prompt to your clipboard that turns the current outline into a styled draw.io flowchart when pasted into Claude or any other AI assistant. Lines ending in "?" are flagged as decision points so the result gets diamonds/branches instead of plain boxes; the prompt also pins exact colors and styling to cut down on manual touch-ups afterward. If clipboard access is blocked, a dialog shows the prompt in a text box to copy manually instead
  • Save AI Diagram (Export ▾ → AI) — paste the AI's reply to the Diagram ✦ prompt here to turn it into a real .drawio file. Code fences and any stray sentences the AI adds before/after the XML are stripped automatically; if what you pasted isn't actually valid draw.io XML, it tells you why instead of saving a broken file
  • Ctrl/Cmd + Shift + D / Insert Date/Time in toolbar → timestamp
  • Copy Tree as Image → PNG
  • Ctrl/Cmd + Shift + C → copy selection
  • Clear → reset
  • Shortcuts that double as OS/browser bindings can occasionally be intercepted before they reach the page — if one doesn't fire, the matching button or menu item always works

Maximize / Zen mode

  • ⤢ floating button bottom-right of the editor, Ctrl/Cmd + Shift + M to toggle it on or off, or Esc to exit only → by default hides the sidebar and app bar, so only the tree, toolbar (if it's on), tab bar, and a trimmed status bar remain
  • Settings → Zen mode → "Hide File Explorer (sidebar)" — on by default, so the sidebar auto-collapses when you enter zen mode. The header's own sidebar-toggle button (▤) keeps working normally throughout zen mode regardless of this setting, so you can always open or close it manually for that session — same "manual choice sticks until you exit" behavior as the toolbar and Pad below
  • Settings → Zen mode → "Hide status bar" controls whether the bottom status bar is hidden outright on entry — off by default. Left on, zen mode instead trims it down to just Words, Depth, Saved status, and the auto-rewrite indicator (plus Quick Assist, if it's normally docked in the app bar — see below), regardless of your normal status bar item selection, and restores your normal selection when you exit
  • Settings → Zen mode → "Hide tab bar" controls whether the row of open document tabs is hidden on entry — off by default, so you can switch between open documents without leaving zen mode. Turn it on if you'd rather it disappear too
  • A second floating button sits next to it — a shortcut for Settings → Bars & Menus → Toolbar → "Show toolbar," so it works the same way whether you're in zen mode or not. Click it, or Ctrl/Cmd + Shift + T, to show or hide the toolbar; the choice applies everywhere and is remembered next time
  • If you'd rather zen mode hide the toolbar by default, turn "Show toolbar" off before maximizing — everything it holds is still reachable by keyboard shortcut (see the other sections on this page) or by right-clicking a node for the icon row and "More" menu
  • The Pad panel has its own floating toggle next to the maximize button, since it stays open across zen mode
  • Settings → Zen mode → "Hide app bar" also hides the very top row (Documents/Templates, search, Settings, Help, Import/Export) while zen — on by default. If Quick Assist is normally docked in the app bar ("Dock in app bar" in Settings → Quick Assist), zen mode relocates it into the status bar for as long as zen mode is on instead, rather than popping the app bar back open every time you invoke it; your "Dock in app bar" preference itself is untouched and Quick Assist returns to the app bar when you exit zen mode. If the app bar is hidden, right-click the editor canvas for a "Show app bar" option (only appears while it's actually hidden), or use the keyboard shortcuts for whatever you need — everything the app bar holds still works that way too
  • Settings → Zen mode → "Use browser fullscreen" — on by default — additionally requests the browser's own fullscreen (the same as pressing F11) when you enter zen mode, hiding the browser's tabs and address bar too, not just Sakura's own UI. Exiting fullscreen — Esc, F11, or the browser's own control — exits zen mode at the same time, so the two never end up out of sync

Preview

  • 👁 floating button bottom-right of the editor (next to zen/Pad/toolbar) → opens a read-only, presentation-style render of the current document. Nothing here can be edited, and closing it never changes your actual document
  • Headings come from the Heading 1–6 style you apply in the editor (Format toolbar, next to Text color) — not from tree depth. A node with no heading style of its own renders as plain body text in Preview, unless Settings → Preview → "Heading fallback by depth" has a level assigned to that node's depth (see below)
  • Settings → Preview → "Heading fallback by depth" lets you assign a heading level to depths 0–5, so an unstyled node can still render as a heading in Preview based purely on how deep it sits in the tree. An explicit heading style on the node always wins over this; depths left as "None" behave exactly as before. This only changes how Preview renders — it never writes a heading style into the actual document
  • Table of contents (left panel, toggled with the header button) is built from headings and [Section] markers — what it includes is configurable in Settings → Preview → "Table of contents": cap it to a maximum heading level, or turn off section markers to keep it to real headings only. Below the headings, a "Decisions" section lists every decision log in the document with a colored status dot — click to jump straight to it. The panel shows itself if there are 2+ headings or at least 1 decision log, whichever applies; it's part of what gets printed to PDF, so the decision index carries over there too
  • Presenter mode (▶ button in the header) goes fullscreen and hides all the header buttons — the bottom bar (prev/next, slide counter, exit) is the only control surface while presenting. Steps through the document one section at a time — arrow keys/space to navigate, Home/End to jump to first/last, Esc (or leaving fullscreen any other way) to exit. Fold state, notes, code blocks, and decision cards all render normally within each slide. Which tree depth starts a new slide, and whether the laser pointer turns on automatically with presenter mode, are both configurable in Settings → Preview → Appearance & PDF
  • Settings → Preview → "Appearance" adds reading width, a sepia reading theme, and a serif body-text option — Preview-only, doesn't touch the editor or document. "PDF page margins" there sets the default Export as PDF starts with; the print dialog that opens still lets you change it per export
  • Click ▾/▸ → fold/unfold a branch, same as the editor — but this fold state is local to Preview and separate from the editor's own collapsed sections. Collapse all / Expand all buttons sit in the header
  • Folding a branch that contains a note or a decision log doesn't hide it silently — a small icon next to the +N badge tells you it's in there; click either to expand
  • Notes render as their real rich content (images, lists, links) inside a card, not the small dot from the editor
  • Decision logs become a card with a "Decision Log" header — Status always shows there as a colored badge — including its unedited default of "Proposed", since that's a real status rather than a placeholder question (color is guessed from the word itself: approved/accepted/implemented/resolved/done/confirmed → green, rejected/declined/blocked/abandoned/cancelled → red, hold/pending/review → orange, anything else → gray). Each other answered field gets its own labeled block below, in order — and if you've written the actual answer as child nodes under a field (e.g. a numbered list under Rationale, or bullet points under Impact) rather than on the field's own line, that nested content renders as a list inside the block, numbered automatically if your items start with "1.", "2.", etc. A field only shows if it has real content — either its own line has been edited past the default question, or it has children. Which fields appear at all is configurable in Settings → Preview → "Decision card fields" (Context + Decision by default; this only affects how Preview summarizes a log, not what the Decision Log feature itself inserts)
  • Right-click anywhere in Preview → Laser pointer, for pointing at things while presenting without your real cursor in the way. Turns off automatically when you close Preview
  • Export buttons in the header (Markdown / Word) are the same export as everywhere else in Sakura — Preview just gives you a one-click path to them while you're already looking at the rendered result

Large trees

  • Click ▾/▸ → fold / unfold subtree
  • ← / → → collapse / expand selected
  • Ctrl/Cmd + F → search this tree; Ctrl/Cmd + H → search and replace (Replace / Replace all, undoable)
  • Ctrl/Cmd + Alt + C → collapse all
  • Ctrl/Cmd + Alt + E / ▼ in toolbar → expand all
  • ▶ in toolbar → collapse to a set depth (configurable in Settings)

Documents & folders (sidebar)

  • button in sidebar header → new folder; button → new document (unfiled); or Ctrl/Cmd + Alt + N from anywhere
  • Hover a folder → ＋ creates a document already filed there; ✎ renames it; deletes it (subfolders move up a level, documents move to Unfiled — Undo appears briefly after deleting)
  • Right-click a folder → new document or new subfolder here, rename, or delete
  • Right-click a document → rename, duplicate, move to a folder, or move to Trash
  • Click ✎ / ⧉ / icons on a document row → rename / duplicate / move to Trash; the folder-icon button moves it via a dropdown list
  • Click a document's icon (left of its name) → pick from a small set of icons to mark it visually, or "Use default icon" to clear it back to the plain page icon. Purely visual, doesn't affect the document's content
  • Click a document's or folder's name → switch to it / expand it
  • Folders can be nested: drag a folder onto another folder's top or bottom edge to reorder it as a sibling there, or onto the middle to nest it inside
  • Drag a document onto a folder (or "Unfiled") to file it there; drag it onto another document to reorder within that folder/Unfiled
  • / buttons by "Documents" → collapse all / expand all folders (only shown once a folder exists)
  • Ctrl/Cmd-click a document to multi-select; Shift-click selects a range. A bar appears to move or trash the whole selection at once
  • Deleting a document (or a selection of them) — or a template, including a built-in one — moves it to Trash, a collapsible section at the very bottom of the sidebar, below Templates — out of the way since it's rarely needed, same as most apps put it. It's not gone. Each item there shows when it was trashed, with buttons to restore it or delete it forever; documents and templates sit in the same list, told apart by their icon and a small "Template" label. A toast with "Undo" also appears right after trashing, for a quick reversal without opening Trash at all. Settings → Trash controls how long items sit there before being purged automatically (default 7 days; "Forever" turns off auto-purge so only "Empty Trash" removes anything) — one setting, shared by both. Header search (Ctrl/Cmd + Space) skips Trash by default — turn on Settings → Trash → "Include Trash in search" to have it surface matches there too (across documents, templates, node text, Notes, tags, and the Pad alike), clearly marked "In Trash"; clicking one reveals it in the Trash section rather than opening it directly, since restoring is a deliberate choice. Settings → Data → "Restore built-in templates" also pulls a trashed built-in back out of Trash, not just refreshes its content
  • Searching the sidebar keeps folders visible (showing only matching documents and subfolders, force-expanded) but disables drag-and-drop while a search is active; it only matches document/template names — for text inside documents, use the header search (Ctrl/Cmd + Space) instead
  • The sidebar's filter box is hidden by default since the header search now covers the same ground; bring it back via the icon next to "Documents", or Settings → Bars & Menus → Sidebar → Filter box
  • Drag the thin strip at the sidebar's right edge to resize it
  • Version history (right-click a document → "Version history", or "More" → "Version history" for the one currently open) keeps automatic checkpoints of a document's past content — not full version control, just lightweight snapshots. A version is captured right before it would otherwise be overwritten, but only once every ~10 minutes of active editing at most, and only if something actually changed, so it won't flood with near-duplicates from every autosave tick. Capped at the last 20 per document, oldest dropped first. "Save a version now" in that panel adds one immediately, useful right before a big rewrite. Restoring an older version is itself non-destructive — whatever was there gets saved as a version first, so restoring is never a one-way trip
  • If the same document is open as the active one in two tabs or windows of this browser at once (easy to do by accident — duplicating a tab, browser session restore, opening the file twice), a banner appears the moment the other one saves a change, since otherwise whichever tab saves last would silently win with no warning. "Reload latest" discards anything unsaved here and pulls in the other tab's version; dismissing it just clears the banner — your edits here are kept either way, and the other tab gets the same warning in reverse once you save. Documents open here only in the background (not the one on screen) refresh silently instead, since there's nothing unsaved there to protect. This only applies within one browser — a completely different browser, or a different device, never shares storage and can't collide

Templates (sidebar)

  • Click a template's name in the Templates section → load it into the current document (replaces current content; Undo restores it)
  • Default → loads your custom default template, if one is set
  • ⋯ button → Save current as template, or Set current as default
  • button next to ⋯ → new template folder, for grouping templates (single level; templates can be moved between folders via the move-to-folder button or right-click, but folders themselves can't be nested or reordered)
  • / buttons by "Templates" → collapse all / expand all template folders (only shown once a template folder exists)
  • Click a template's icon → same small icon-picker as documents, to mark it visually. Built-in templates keep their fixed icon (grouped by document shape) and aren't customizable this way
  • Ships with a built-in pack — Meeting Notes, 1:1 Notes, Daily Standup, Weekly Status, Decision Record, Project Kickoff, Retrospective — grouped in a "Built-in" folder. The folder and the templates in it can be renamed and moved like any other, but are deleted like any other — deletion goes to Trash and is reversible. Use the ↺ restore button in the Templates sidebar header to restore any deleted or renamed built-in templates at any time. Icons are grouped by document shape, not unique per template: calendar for anything written on a recurring cadence, checkmark for the decision record, flag for one-off project milestones
  • The "Templates ▾" button in the header toolbar opens the same template list as a quick-access dropdown, grouped by folder exactly like the sidebar (icons included) — useful when the sidebar itself is hidden, e.g. in zen mode.
