It's read-mostly there: click a template to load it, plus rename/move-to-folder/delete per row, but folders themselves are only created from the sidebar
  • A line that's exactly "Date:", "Author:", or "Date · Author" in any template (built-in or your own) gets auto-filled the moment the template loads — today's date, and the document's current author if one is set (see Settings → Editing → "Default author" to pre-fill that automatically too). The merged "Date · Author" form fills in with no labels, just the values
  • Built-in templates use one color convention throughout, worth keeping if you duplicate and extend one: purple = neutral section header, orange = action/decision, red = risk/blocker, green = positive
  • Want a template in a new doc instead of overwriting? Create a new document first, then load the template

Tabs

  • Click a document in the sidebar, or the + at the end of the tab strip → opens it as a tab. Switching tabs keeps each one's undo history, scroll position, and selection independent — it doesn't reset like a plain document switch would
  • Double-click a tab → rename it
  • ✕ on a tab, or Ctrl/Cmd + Alt + W → closes the active tab only; the document itself isn't deleted and stays in the sidebar. Closing the last tab leaves the editor empty rather than reopening another document
  • Drag a tab left or right to reorder the strip
  • ▾ at the right of the tab strip → search all open tabs by name; ↑ / ↓ + Enter to jump, or click a result
  • Settings → Workspace → "Reopen tabs on launch" controls whether your open tabs come back next time, or you start with just one

Backing up your data

  • Everything is stored locally in the browser, scoped to this exact file. Opening a different copy or a newer version of this file starts with empty storage — that's a browser limitation, not a bug
  • Settings → Data → Auto-backup to file → keeps a live backup written to a file on disk as you work (Chrome/Edge only — Safari and Firefox don't support this yet). Browsers revoke write permission to that file across a reload or restart as a deliberate security measure, so this can need reconnecting — a chip in the status bar flags it when that happens, and clicking it (or "Reconnect" in Settings) restores it in one click. If you disconnect it yourself from Settings, that same chip stays visible with a plain "Connect backup" prompt rather than disappearing, so there's always a way back to it from the status bar
  • Settings → Data → Local safety copy → a second copy kept automatically in a separate browser storage area; Restore brings it back if the main storage gets cleared. Not a substitute for Export — it's still inside the same browser
  • Settings → Data → Backup / Restore → still the way to move data between files, browsers, or computers, or to take a one-off manual backup; the sidebar footer has Backup/Restore shortcuts too, so you don't need to open Settings just for that. The header's Export ▾ and Import ▾ buttons are for the current document only (Markdown, Word, OPML, etc.) — Backup/Restore cover all your data
  • Settings → Data → "Warn if this session isn't backed up" → offers Export or Connect auto-backup if you've made changes this session but neither has run yet. Three modes: Strict shows the browser's own "leave site?" prompt when you try to close the tab, then this dialog if you cancel it — a real last-chance gate, at the cost of that generic browser popup (its wording is fixed by the browser, not by Sakura). Passive skips the browser prompt entirely and shows only this dialog, the next time you switch away from or close the tab — quieter, but a direct close with no prior tab-switch gets no warning at all. Off disables both; the status-bar "⚠ Back up your data" chip (from the reminder setting above) is your only remaining signal
  • Recommended: export before switching to a new version of this file, then import right after opening it
  • Settings → Data → Reset to default settings → restores appearance/behavior preferences only (theme, toolbar, right-click menu, etc.); your documents and AI settings aren't touched

Note panel

  • Every node can hold a rich-text note — open it via the toolbar ( Note button), right-click → More → Note, or Ctrl/Cmd + Shift + N; clicking the chat-bubble dot that appears on a node with a note also opens it directly
  • The note panel is a floating, draggable window — drag it by its header to reposition it anywhere; click to expand to full-screen and click again to restore
  • Compact view — click the compact icon next to Maximize to shrink the note to a minimal popover: just the editor plus Rewrite and Clear (relocated into the header row), with backlinks, timestamps, and the rest of the toolbar hidden. Click it again to expand back to the full view. Compact and Maximize are mutually exclusive — switching to either one always drops out of the other, since a fullscreen note with only Rewrite, or a tiny popover with the full toolbar, isn't a real state. Settings → Note panel → "Open notes in compact view" sets whether newly-opened notes start compact by default; expanding an individual note back to full view is a one-off for that note and doesn't change the default
  • Toolbar: B I U S for bold / italic / underline / strikethrough; bullet list and numbered list; insert or edit a link, with its own display-text field separate from the URL; table icon inserts a 2×2 table; AI Rewrite rewrites the whole note or just selected text; the summarise button reads the node's child subtree and appends a prose summary to the note
  • Tab → indents a list item (when inside a list) or inserts spaces in plain text; Shift + Tab → outdent a list item
  • Paste images directly into the note — they embed inline and are stored with the note. Click a pasted image to select it, then drag the handle at its corner to resize; double-click it to reset back to its original size
  • Tables: drag the thin border handle between columns to resize column widths
  • Ctrl/Cmd + Enter or Esc → close and save; "Clear note" removes it entirely (undoable)
  • A chat-bubble dot on a node row signals it has a note — click it to open that note; this also selects that node
  • Backlinks — if other nodes reference the current node via @mention, a Backlinks section appears at the bottom of the note panel. Click any entry to jump to that referencing node
  • Timestamps — the note panel also shows when the node was created and last modified, on a single line at the bottom. Existing nodes show the time they were first loaded after this feature was added; new nodes and any node whose text is edited going forward will have accurate timestamps

Code Block

  • Every node can hold one code block — open it via the toolbar ( Code block button), right-click → More → Code block…, or Ctrl/Cmd + Shift + K; clicking the </> dot that appears on a node with a code block also opens it directly
  • The code panel is a floating, draggable window, anchored next to the selected node the same way the note panel is — drag it by its header to reposition it anywhere; click the maximize icon to expand to full-screen and click again to restore
  • A real <textarea> for editing — no rich-text formatting, just plain code. Drag the bottom edge to resize it vertically
  • Language picker: Plain text, ABAP, SQL, JavaScript, Python, JSON, XML/HTML, Markdown
  • Highlight toggles between raw edit mode and a read-only syntax-highlighted view — the raw text in the textarea is always what's saved, the highlighted view is just for reading
  • Copy copies the raw code text (not HTML) to your clipboard
  • Ctrl/Cmd + Enter or Esc → close and save; "Clear code" removes it entirely (undoable)
  • A </> dot on a node row signals it has a code block — click it to open that code block without changing the selection

Decision Log

  • Inserts a structured record under the selected node: a header line (timestamp + optional author) with child prompts for Context, Decision, Rationale, Alternatives, Impact, and Status
  • Insert via the toolbar's Decision Log button (in the Insert group), or right-click a node → More → Decision log
  • Settings → Decision Log → "Ask who decided" prompts for a name each time (on by default); the timestamp is always added regardless. "Highlight field labels" colors the Context/Decision/Rationale/etc. labels in the editor so they stand out from the rest of the text — it doesn't extend to the Decision Log card in Preview, which keeps its labels neutral on purpose so the colored Status badge is the only thing carrying color in that card
  • Settings → Decision Log → "Fields to include" lets you turn off any of the six prompts you don't use — e.g. skip Alternatives, Impact, and Status if you only ever fill in Context, Decision, and Rationale. The header itself always gets added; this only controls which child prompts come with it. Changes apply to logs you insert afterward, not ones already in a document
  • A small checkmark icon appears next to the "+N hidden" badge on a folded node when something inside its collapsed subtree contains a decision log, so you know there's one there before expanding
  • Export menu → Decision Log → Excel (.xlsx) pulls every decision log across the current document into one spreadsheet (timestamp, author, parent node, and whichever fields were filled in) — fields you've turned off, or left blank, just export as empty cells

Pad

  • A docked panel for whole-document notes — separate from per-node Notes above. One Pad per document, not tied to any single node
  • Open it with the small panel icon docked at the bottom-right of the editor, next to the maximize button, or Ctrl/Cmd + Shift + P; the same shortcut and button close it again. It stays open if the editor is maximized, and that floating button is how you open or close it while maximized too — handy if Settings → Bars & Menus → Toolbar → "Show toolbar" is off and the regular toolbar isn't around to use instead
  • Drag the thin strip on its left edge to resize it; width is remembered, as is whether it's open or closed
  • Settings → Pad → "Notepad" toggle hides the panel and its floating toggle button entirely if you don't use it (on by default)
  • Settings → Pad → "Header" (off by default) adds a title bar with its own close button — turning it on hides the toolbar's close button so there's only ever one; "Match editor background" controls whether Pad's writing surface looks like the outline's canvas or reads as visually distinct chrome — personal preference, no right answer
  • Settings → Pad → "Toolbar buttons" lets you hide individual buttons (Bold, Table, AI Rewrite, etc.) you don't use
  • Toolbar mirrors the Note panel: B I U S, bullet / numbered lists, link (with its own display-text field, separate from the URL), table insert with resizable columns, image paste/insert (click to select, drag the corner handle to resize, double-click to reset), and AI Rewrite (rewrites the selection, or the whole Pad if nothing's selected). No subtree-summarise here — there's no subtree at the document level
  • "Clear pad" permanently removes all content for the current document, after a confirmation prompt — unlike the per-node Note's clear, this isn't on the outline's Ctrl/Cmd+Z undo stack, since Pad content isn't part of that snapshot

To-Dos

  • An app-level task list — not tied to any document, node, or Pad. One shared list across your whole workspace
  • Open it from the app bar (or the status bar, if you've moved it there) with Ctrl/Cmd + Shift + U; the same shortcut closes it again
  • Floating and draggable by its header; drag the thin strip on its left edge to resize its width (remembered between sessions, same as its position). The button expands it full-screen the same way Note/Pad/Code do (temporarily overriding your custom width, restored when you leave full-screen); the button next to it exports the whole list as plain text
  • Type a task and press Enter (or use the + button) to add it — the box grows as you type so long tasks stay readable. Shift+Enter inserts a line break instead of adding the task, both here and while editing an existing task's text
  • Checking a task off moves it straight to Completed, most-recently-finished first; unchecking sends it back to Open
  • The Open row shows how many open tasks match the current filter, with a magnifying-glass quick-find icon, sort and filter icons at its right edge. Sort covers Manual order, Priority, and Due date; filter narrows the list to one priority level (or tasks with none). Any icon turns accent-colored whenever it's off its default (or quick-find is open) so you can tell at a glance that something's active
  • Click the magnifying-glass icon to open quick-find and type to search task text, across both Open and Completed. Matches are highlighted and non-matching tasks are hidden entirely (not just dimmed), so the list actually shortens as you narrow it down — this composes with the priority filter, so a priority filter plus a search term shows only tasks matching both. Esc clears the search text first, then a second Esc (or clicking the icon again) closes it; the search always resets when you close the panel. Dragging to reorder still works correctly while filtered — a task dropped next to a visible neighbor lands there in the full list too, even if other hidden tasks sit between them. This searches task text only, live, within the panel — for a version that also opens the panel and jumps to the match from anywhere in the app, Quick Assist's todo: scope still does that
  • Drag the handle that appears on hover to reorder open tasks. Completed tasks aren't draggable — they're always ordered by completion time
  • Hover a task for a spark AI Rewrite button and a delete button — both sit as an overlay rather than reserving space, so task text keeps the full row width when you're not hovering. Deleting shows an "Undo" toast, inside the panel itself, for a few seconds in case of a misclick
  • The Completed section header shows a Clear completed button whenever it has tasks (respecting the current priority filter) to remove them all in one action, with its own in-panel Undo toast — a batch counterpart to deleting one at a time. All To-Dos toasts (Undo prompts, export confirmation, AI rewrite errors) show inside the panel while it's open rather than the app's global toast, so acting on one never closes the panel
  • An overdue count badge can appear on the To-Dos button itself (app bar or status bar) whenever you have open, overdue tasks — visible without opening the panel. Toggle it off at Settings → To-Dos → "Overdue count badge" if you'd rather not see it
  • Priority, due date, and link chips sit below the task text. Priority cycles None → Low → Medium → High on click; due date shows "Today/Tomorrow" or the date, turning red once it's overdue. All three stay visible once set, and only reveal on hover while unset. Clicking the due date chip opens a small calendar popover with Today / Tomorrow / Next week presets up top for the common cases, month navigation for picking any other date, and a "Clear due date" option. Clicking the link chip opens a small popover with an optional display-text field, a URL field, a Save button, and a Remove link option; a bare domain or path is treated as an https:// link automatically, and the chip shows your display text if you set one, or the link's hostname otherwise. Once a link is attached, an open-link button appears in the row's hover actions, next to Rewrite and Delete, to open it in a new tab without going through the editor popover
  • Typing @ directly in a task's text renders an inline chip: @name for an assignee, @date (ISO date, @today/@tomorrow, or a weekday like @fri) for a date reference, and @status (waiting/blocked/done/review) for status. A suggestion popup appears as you type, offering the date/status keywords plus any names you've already used as @name elsewhere — Arrow keys to navigate, Enter/Tab to accept, Escape to dismiss. These are colored text only — display convenience within the task text, not linked to the priority/due-date/link fields above
  • #tag renders as a colored tag chip — same autocomplete popup, suggesting tags you've already used. >remark renders everything from the > to the end of the line as a muted italic aside. Both are display only, same as @ tokens
  • Quick-find already searches the raw task text, so it matches #tags, @mentions, and remark text without any special handling — priority stays a separate structured field with its own dedicated filter
  • Settings → To-Dos: dock the toggle button in the status bar instead of the app bar; show or hide the overdue count badge; auto-expire completed tasks after a set number of days; cap how many completed tasks are kept (oldest drop first)
  • Quick Assist searches your tasks too — type a task's text, or scope it with todo: milk — and clicking a result opens the panel and flashes the match

Feature activation

  • Settings → Features — on/off switches for the handful of capabilities most people actually turn off (Note, Code, Tagging, AI, Pad, To-Dos, Quick Assist). Panel-specific extras like Decision Log, Focus, Backlinks, Hover Toolbar, and Node Quick Assist have their own enable switch inside that panel's own Settings section instead — see each one's Help entry below for details. A small dot appears next to "Features" in the rail when any capability here is off, as a reminder
  • Note Panel — per-node rich-text notes. Off: the note toolbar button, right-click action, note dot on nodes, and the note panel itself are all hidden. Existing note content is preserved — turning it back on restores everything
  • Pad Panel — a docked whole-document notepad, separate from per-node notes. Off: the floating Pad toggle button (bottom-right of the editor) and the Settings → Pad section are hidden. The Pad's content is preserved
  • To-Dos — the app-level task list, not tied to any document. Off: the app bar/status bar To-Dos button, its panel, and the Settings → To-Dos section are hidden. Existing tasks are preserved
  • AI Capabilities — the Rewrite, Expand, Summarise, Generate outline, Suggest icon, Suggest tags, and Restructure text (Import menu) features. Off: the AI toolbar group, all matching right-click items, the "Restructure text" entry in Import ▾, and every section under Settings → AI are hidden. Word import (Import ▾ → Word) still works while this is off, but loses its automatic AI fallback for documents that have no heading styles to extract — those import as a flat list instead. Requires a provider API key (Settings → AI → Provider) before anything will run — select a provider and the key hint shows where to get one
  • Tagging — #tag chips on nodes. Off: the tag toolbar button, right-click action, and tag chips on all nodes are hidden. Tags are still stored in the document data; turning the feature back on restores them
  • Quick Assist — the search box in the status bar (Ctrl/Cmd+Space), which handles both plain-English commands and general search in one place. Off: the shortcut does nothing, the box disappears from the status bar, and any open dropdown closes immediately. Search-result behavior inside it (on/off, category filter, result cap) has its own dedicated section at Settings → Workspace → Quick Assist
  • Turning a feature off hides every entry point for it (toolbar, right-click, Settings sub-section, Help sub-section). The underlying data is never deleted — toggling a feature is non-destructive in both directions. Feature states are saved alongside your other preferences and restored on the next launch
Start with structure. Refine later.
From a file
From pasted text
Copy
Document
Data
AI
Right‑click a folder or doc for more options. Drag rows to move or reorder them.

This document changed in another tab.

🌸
Sakura in action
Like a cherry blossom tree, every idea here grows as a branch — nested, structured, beautiful.
We're going to build out a real document: a "Product launch plan" outline with Marketing and Engineering branches. Along the way you'll see formatting, an AI rewrite, a note, a decision log, a quick Pad reminder, then a preview and a Word export — all on that one plan. Press Start when you're ready.
🌸
The design philosophy behind Sakura
Simplicity is the ultimate sophistication.
— Leonardo da Vinci
Start your own — every idea grows as a branch.
Laser pointer✓
🌸 Sakura

Welcome — where would you like to start?

Pick how you want to get familiar. You can always revisit both from the Help menu.

🌸Sakura

Why an outliner — and why Sakura?

Think of an outliner as turning your notes into a family tree, instead of a messy pile of paragraphs. Instead of writing left to right, you write in points that tuck inside one another. Here's why that makes life easier:

See the big picture without the clutter Five pages of notes usually means scrolling forever to find what you want. In Sakura, you can collapse the details and see just your main headings — like a table of contents. Click one open when you need it.
Move things around like LEGO blocks In a normal document, moving a section means highlighting, cutting, pasting, and fixing the spacing. Here you just grab one point and drag it — everything tucked underneath comes with it automatically.
Bring in the mess, get back structure Paste in messy notes or import a Word document — Sakura sorts it into a tree for you, AI-assisted when it needs to be. This part's specific to Sakura, not outliners in general.
It keeps you thinking, not just typing Plain note-taking lets you write down what someone said without really processing it. An outliner makes you decide, in the moment: is this a new idea, or a detail under the last one? That keeps you actively sorting instead of just transcribing.
Not every kind of writing wants this. Flowing essays and loosely connected notes are still better off elsewhere — Sakura's built for things that have a shape: plans, specs, architecture, meeting notes, decisions.
Version History —
Keeps the last 20 versions per document
No tags yet

Toolbar Buttons

Choose which icon groups and individual buttons appear on the main toolbar
Hide groups you don't use — keyboard-first workflows often skip History or Structure
Hiding the whole History section above also hides everything below — use this instead to keep one and drop the other
Hiding the whole Structure section above also hides everything below — use this instead to keep some Structure buttons and drop others
Hiding the whole Move section above also hides everything below — use this instead to keep one and drop the other
Hiding the whole Format section above also hides everything below — use this instead to keep some Format buttons and drop others
Hiding the whole Fold section above also hides everything below — use this instead to keep some Fold buttons and drop others
Hiding the whole Insert section above also hides everything below — use this instead to keep some Insert buttons and drop others, e.g. keep Note & Checkbox but hide Decision Log & Tags
Hiding the whole AI section above also hides everything below — use this instead to keep some AI buttons and drop others
Same effect as hiding the whole Delete section above — included here for consistency with the other groups
Hiding the whole Extras section above also hides everything below — use this instead to keep some Extras actions and drop others (applies to both the Extras menu and the buttons shown when Expanded is on)

Right-click Menu

Choose which actions appear when you right-click a node
Pick which actions show as icons at the top of a node's right-click menu — everything else stays one click away under "More". Sort children always stays under More since it's a group, not a single action.
Turn off an action here to remove it from the right-click menu entirely — quick row and "More" both. Unlike "Quick row" above, this isn't about placement, it's full removal.

Keyboard Shortcuts

Click a combo to change it · Esc cancels · needs Ctrl/Cmd or Alt
No matching shortcuts
Arrow keys, Tab, Enter/F2, and Delete are core tree navigation and aren't customizable here.

Sakura Cheatsheet

Everything at a glance — full detail lives in Help
No matching shortcuts

The Basics

  • Enter/F2 edit node
  • Enter new sibling (while editing)
  • Shift+Enter split at cursor
  • new child
  • Tab/Shift+Tab indent / outdent
  • / move node
  • ←/→ collapse / expand
  • Click select · Shift+click range · Ctrl/Cmd+click multi
  • Right-click → icon row (Zoom in, Note, Tags, Rewrite, Decision Log, Delete by default) + More for move, sort, and everything else; customisable in Settings → Right-click menu

Semantics

  • [Text] → Section label
  • (Text) → note muted italic
  • !Text → alert red warning
  • `Text` → code inline code
  • >Text → quoted content — attribution, citation, direct quote
  • @Node name → clickable backlink (type @ while editing to pick)
  • [ ] text or [x] text → auto-converts to checkbox on commit
  • Outline numbering — Settings → Editing → "Outline numbering" (1, 1.1, 1.1.1)

Formatting

  • bold
  • italic
  • underline
  • strike
  • highlight
  • text color
  • –6 heading 1–6 (independent of depth)
  • [Text] → Section · (Text) → note
  • !Text → alert · `Text` → code
  • @Node name → backlink (type @ while editing to pick)
  • [ ] or [x] at start → auto-converts to checkbox on commit

Toolbar

  • Undo / Redo — /
  • Add child —
  • Select all —
  • B I U S, highlight, color — text styling
  • Collapse to depth — or ▶ button (depth set in Settings)
  • Expand all —
  • Intelligent paste — parses indents/bullets into nodes
  • Insert Date/Time —
  • Note —
  • Decision Log — Insert group

Search & Quick Assist

  • Quick Assist — plain-English commands, or search anything
  • While editing a node, the same shortcut opens a smaller node-specific menu instead (em dash, date/time, note, code block, tags, decision log, rewrite) — see Node Quick Assist below
  • Covers settings, help, doc & template names, node text, notes, code blocks, tags, and the pad — across all documents
  • Results group by type ("In documents", "Notes", "Code", "Tags", "Pad") with a snippet — click to jump straight there
  • search this tree
  • search & replace
  • Sidebar filter only matches doc/template names — use header search for text inside docs

Pad & Notes

  • Note — per-node, rich text ·
  • Floating, draggable, to expand full-screen
  • Pad — one per document, not tied to a node ·
  • Both: B I U S, lists, link, table, image paste, AI rewrite
  • Note adds a subtree-summarise button; Pad doesn't (no subtree)
  • "Clear pad" isn't on the undo stack — per-node note clear is
  • Note panel shows Backlinks — nodes that @mention this one (click to jump)
  • Note panel shows Created / Modified timestamps for the node

To-Dos

  • App-level task list — not tied to any document or node ·
  • Floating, draggable, to expand full-screen
  • Check off a task to move it to Completed (newest first); drag the hover handle to reorder Open tasks
  • Priority and due-date chips below each task; both auto-hide until set or hovered
  • Type @ in task text for inline chips, with autocomplete — @name assignee, @date (e.g. @tomorrow, @fri), @status (waiting/blocked/done/review) — display only
  • # for a #tag (autocompletes from prior tags), > for a muted remark to end of line — both display only
  • Quick-find matches #tags, @mentions, and remark text too — it's a plain substring search over the task text
  • Settings → To-Dos: app bar or status bar placement, auto-expiry, and a cap on completed tasks
  • Searchable from Quick Assist — plain text or todo: milk

Code Block

  • One per node, raw <textarea> editing — no rich text ·
  • 8 languages: Plain text, ABAP, SQL, JavaScript, Python, JSON, XML/HTML, Markdown
  • Floating, draggable, anchors next to the node like the Note panel; expand full-screen the same way
  • Highlight toggle: raw edit ↔ read-only syntax-highlighted view; raw text is always what's saved
  • Copy button copies raw code text, not HTML
  • A </> dot on a node row signals it has a code block — click to open

AI Features

  • Rewrite — selected node(s), or highlighted text
  • Right-click → More → Rewrite document for the whole tree
  • Generate outline ·
  • Restructure text — Import menu → paste messy/unstructured text → organized into nodes, with wording cleaned up as needed to read well as an outline (won't invent facts not in the source); always opens as a new document, original text kept in its Pad for reference; already-structured pastes (bullets/indents) skip the AI call ·
  • Word import — Import menu → .docx file: uses the file's own heading/list styles directly, no AI needed when they're present; falls back to AI restructuring only when the document has no heading styles at all. Tables become a node per row with cells as children; images become a "[image]" placeholder. Always opens as a new document
  • Suggest icon — picks a fitting emoji prefix
  • Batches over 30 nodes send in sequential chunks
  • Expand node — AI toolbar → expand button (single node selected) → breaks into child nodes
  • Suggest tags — AI toolbar → tag button → applies relevant tags from existing doc tags or new ones
  • Summarise selection — select 2+ nodes → AI toolbar → summarise button → inserts a parent label above them
  • Settings → AI → Provider: choose from six built-in providers (Gemini, Groq, Claude, OpenRouter, Cerebras, GitHub Models) or add your own — paste your API key; all AI features share the same active provider

Export

  • copy as text
  • copy selection as image
  • Markdown .md
  • Tree .txt
  • Word .docx
  • Decision Log → Excel .xlsx
  • Export ▾ / Import ▾ in header — document-level (Markdown, Word, OPML…)
  • Sidebar footer → Backup / Restore — full app data

Docs, Tabs & Zen

  • new document
  • Click a doc or template's icon in the sidebar → pick from a small icon set (built-in templates excluded)
  • close tab
  • save now
  • toggle zen mode
  • Esc exit zen mode only (doesn't toggle back in)
  • Preview — read-only render; foldable, right-click for a laser pointer
  • toggle toolbar
  • toggle app bar — the reliable way back if you hide it, since Settings lives in that row too

Features

  • Settings → Features — toggle optional capabilities on/off
  • A dot appears next to "Features" in the rail when any capability is off
  • Note Panel — per-node rich text notes
  • Pad Panel — one whole-document notepad per doc
  • AI Capabilities — Rewrite, Expand, Summarise, Outline, Tags, Icon, Restructure text
  • Decision Log — structured decision records + Excel export
  • Tagging — #tag chips on nodes
  • @ Mentions & Backlinks — link nodes; see incoming links in Note panel
  • Focus — zoom into a branch; Ctrl/Cmd + . to enter, Ctrl/Cmd + , to exit
  • Off = hidden, not deleted — data is always preserved; states saved across sessions
  • hide tree lines — clean indented view, like Dynalist

Tabs

  • Click a doc in the sidebar or + at end of strip → open as tab
  • new document
  • close active tab (doc stays in sidebar)
  • Double-click tab → rename · Drag to reorder · ▾ → search open tabs
  • Each tab keeps its own undo history, scroll, and selection
  • Settings → Startup → "Reopen tabs on launch" restores them next session

Version History

  • Right-click a doc in the sidebar → "Version history" → automatic snapshots
  • Snapshot taken before content would be overwritten — at most once per ~10 min, capped at last 20 per document
  • "Save a version now" → snapshot immediately, useful before a big rewrite
  • Restoring saves current content as a version first — always reversible

Shortcuts

  • Settings → Keyboard Shortcuts → Customize — remap any action
  • save now
  • toggle toolbar
  • Quick Assist — commands, or search anything
  • generate outline (AI)
  • restructure text into tree (AI)
  • Shortcuts blocked by OS/browser always have a matching button
Shortcuts blocked by the OS or browser always have a matching button or menu item that works instead.
